column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text==Good Family Bread.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=For five common-sized loaves, make a pint and a half of thin water\r
#Text=gruel.
#Text=Use half a teacupful of fine Indian meal.
#Text=Salt it a little more\r
#Text=than if it were to be eaten as gruel, and boil ten or fifteen minutes.
#Text=This is of importance, as, if the meal is only scalded, the bread will\r
#Text=be coarse.
#Text=Add enough milk to make two quarts of the whole.
#Text=If the milk\r
#Text=is new, the gruel may be poured into it in the pan; if not, it should\r
#Text=be scalded in the kettle with the gruel.
#Text=This is particularly important\r
#Text=in the summer, as at that season milk which is but a few hours old,\r
#Text=and is sweet when put into the bread, will sour in the dough in a\r
#Text=short time.
#Text=When the mixture is cool, so that you are _sure_ it will\r
#Text=not scald, add a teacupful of yeast, and then stir in sifted flour[4]\r
#Text=enough to make a thick batter.
#Text=This is called a sponge.
#Text=This being done\r
#Text=in the evening, let it stand, if in summer, in a cool place, if in\r
#Text=winter, in a moderately warm place, till morning.
#Text=Then add flour enough\r
#Text=to make it easy to mould, and knead it very thoroughly
#Text=.
#Text=[4] All kinds of flour and meal should be sifted for use, except\r
#Text= buckwheat and Graham flour.
#Text=This process of kneading is very important in making bread, and there\r
#Text=are but few domestics whom it is not necessary to instruct how to do\r
#Text=it.
#Text=They generally work over the dough without expending any strength\r
#Text=upon it.
#Text=The hands should be closely shut, and the fists pressed hard\r
#Text=and quickly upon the dough, dipping them into flour whenever the\r
#Text=dough sticks to them.
#Text=A half an hour is the least time to be given to\r
#Text=kneading a baking of bread, unless you prefer, after having done this\r
#Text=till it ceases to stick to your hands, to chop it with a chopping-knife\r
#Text=four or five hundred strokes.
#Text=An hour's kneading is not too much.
#Text=All this looks on paper like a long and troublesome process; but I\r
#Text=venture to say that no lady, after having learned the benefit of it,\r
#Text=will be willing to diminish any portion of the labor and attention\r
#Text=necessary to secure such bread as these directions, observed, will\r
#Text=make.
#Text=Practice will make it easy, and no woman of sense will hesitate\r
#Text=in choosing between sour, tough, ill-baked bread, with heaps of wasted\r
#Text=pieces, a dyspeptic husband, and sickly children on the one hand, and\r
#Text=comfort, economy, and health on the other.
#Text=But to return to the bread.
#Text=After it is thoroughly kneaded, divide it\r
#Text=into four or five equal pieces, and mould according to the form of the\r
#Text=pans in which you bake it.
#Text=These being greased with clean drippings,\r
#Text=put in the dough and set it in the sun or near the fire (according\r
#Text=to the season) to rise.
#Text=Loaves of this size will bake in an hour; if\r
#Text=the oven be rather hot, in a few minutes short of an hour.
#Text=Practice\r
#Text=and good judgment must direct these things.
#Text=If the bread rises rather\r
#Text=slowly, take a dish of warm water and wet the top with your hand.
#Text=When the loaves are baked, do not lay them flat upon the table; good\r
#Text=housewives think it makes them heavy.
#Text=Set them on the side, one against\r
#Text=another, and put a coarse cloth closely over them; this makes the crust\r
#Text=tender by keeping in the steam.
#Text=If bread is baked too hard, wring a\r
#Text=towel in cold water and wrap around it while it is yet hot.
#Text=Care is\r
#Text=necessary that bread does not rise too much, and thus become sour,\r
#Text=especially in warm weather; and even if it does not, the freshness is\r
#Text=lost, and an insipid taste is produced, and it becomes dry sooner by\r
#Text=long rising.
#Text=No exact rule can be given; experience and observation\r
#Text=must teach.
#Text=When dough becomes so light as to run over after being\r
#Text=moulded and put into pans, it is best to mould it again, kneading it\r
#Text=hard two or three minutes, but using as little flour as possible; then\r
#Text=lay it back into the pans, and put it immediately into the oven; this\r
#Text=prevents its being tasteless and dry; it will be perfectly light, but\r
#Text=of a different sort, and much preferred by some persons.
#Text=Some people invariably use saleratus in bread, and there are tables\r
#Text=where the effluvia of this article, and the deep yellow color of the\r
#Text=bread, offend the senses before it is tasted.
#Text=If all the materials\r
#Text=used are good, and the dough has not been permitted to sour, white\r
#Text=bread is far better without saleratus, except that which is made with\r
#Text=water.
#Text=If dough has become sour, a teaspoonful of saleratus for every\r
#Text=quart of the milk or water that was used for wetting the bread, will be\r
#Text=sufficient to correct it.
#Text=The tray or pan in which the bread is made,\r
#Text=should be scalded after being washed, every time it is used, except\r
#Text=in cold weather.
#Text=It is not good economy to buy skimmed milk, as some\r
#Text=persons do, for making bread.
#Text=It renders it tough and indigestible,\r
#Text=if used in the ordinary way.
#Text=In case it is used for this purpose, it\r
#Text=should be boiled, and thickened with a little Indian meal in the same\r
#Text=way, and the same proportions as directed for making gruel, in the\r
#Text=receipt for Good Family Bread.
#Text=Use no water with it.
#Text==Raised Doughnuts.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil a quart of milk, and rub smooth in a little cold milk a large\r
#Text=gill of ground rice; when the milk boils up, stir in the rice and a\r
#Text=little salt.
#Text=Let it boil till it thickens, stirring it two or three\r
#Text=times.
#Text=Pour it, hot, upon a quart of flour; when cool enough, add a\r
#Text=gill of yeast, and flour enough to make it stiff as bread.
#Text=Knead it a\r
#Text=great deal.
#Text=Let it rise over night, and when very light, work in three\r
#Text=quarters of a pound of butter, a pound and a half of sugar beaten in\r
#Text=five eggs, and add nutmeg and lemon, juice and rind.
#Text=Let it rise again,\r
#Text=and then roll out and fry it.
#Text=Light bread dough, which is wet with milk, may be made into plain, or\r
#Text=rich dough-nuts, as preferred, with very little trouble.
#Text=Prepare the\r
#Text=dough as directed in the receipt for rusk, and add two or three eggs,\r
#Text=if convenient.
#Text=It is not necessary.
#Text=ON MAKING PASTRY.
#Text=The flour, as in making bread or cake, should be sifted.
#Text=The\r
#Text=best-looking pastry is made with lard, but it is not so healthy or\r
#Text=good, as that which is made with half or two thirds butter.
#Text=Whichever\r
#Text=you use, rub a third of it into the flour, but do not try to rub out\r
#Text=every lump; the less the hands are used the better.
#Text=Add cold water; in\r
#Text=summer, ice water.
#Text=If your crust is shortened wholly with lard, allow\r
#Text=a teaspoonful of salt to a pound (or quart) of flour, and a small\r
#Text=teaspoonful of saleratus to every three pounds.
#Text=Sprinkle the salt into\r
#Text=the flour, and dissolve the saleratus in the water.
#Text=If butter only or\r
#Text=chiefly is used, omit the saleratus.
#Text=When you have put in the water,\r
#Text=stir it quickly, rather stiff, with a knife.
#Text=Do not mould it; it will\r
#Text=make it tough; but when it is barely stirred together, put it on the\r
#Text=board, roll it out, lay thin shavings of butter on every part, sprinkle\r
#Text=a little flour over it, and roll it out again, then lay on butter as\r
#Text=before.
#Text=To avoid much handling of the crust, roll it so thin that all\r
#Text=the butter will be taken up by two or three times rolling in.
#Text=When\r
#Text=it is all rolled in, fold up the crust in a long roll, and double\r
#Text=it, laying the ends together; then lay it aside, and cut from it for\r
#Text=each pie.
#Text=In rolling out for the plates press the pin equally, so as\r
#Text=to make all parts of the same thickness, and as nearly circular as\r
#Text=possible.
#Text=Have the plates ready buttered, or greased with lard, lay in\r
#Text=the crust, and see that all parts touch the plate.
#Text=Take the dish up on\r
#Text=the palm of the left hand, and with the right trim the edges, holding\r
#Text=the knife under and _aslant_, and so cut the crust that the edge of the\r
#Text=dish will be perfectly covered.
#Text=People differ in regard to the proper\r
#Text=thickness of pie-crust.
#Text=A pie in which the fruit constitutes one third\r
#Text=of the thickness, and the two crusts the other two thirds, although\r
#Text=it may look more elegant, is neither so healthful or good as one made\r
#Text=with thinner crust and plenty of fruit.
#Text=Some fruit requires thicker\r
#Text=crust than others; for apple, peach, and pumpkin it should be thin as\r
#Text=a common earthen plate; for juicy fruits, such as berries, cherries,\r
#Text=currants, plums, and for mince, it should be a little thicker.
#Text=Lay some\r
#Text=of the trimmings round the rim of the plate to make the edge of the pie\r
#Text=handsome, and put the rest by themselves, and when there are enough,\r
#Text=roll them out for an under-crust.
#Text=In making cherries, currants, &c., into pies, use deep dishes, and\r
#Text=be careful not to fill them even full, as the syrup will boil over,\r
#Text=and thus, much of the richness of the pie be lost.
#Text=There is one way\r
#Text=effectually to prevent the loss of syrup.
#Text=After you have laid in the\r
#Text=fruit, or mince, and rolled out the upper-crust, wet the rim of the\r
#Text=under-crust all around with cold water [not omitting a single spot, if\r
#Text=you do the syrup will escape at that spot], and sprinkle a very little\r
#Text=flour upon it, lay the trimming upon the rim, wet and flour that in\r
#Text=the same manner, then lay the upper-crust immediately over, and press\r
#Text=it down gently upon the rim.
#Text=The flour and water act as a paste to\r
#Text=fasten the crusts together.
#Text=Trim the edge as before, and prick the top\r
#Text=eight or ten times with a fork.
#Text=This is necessary for the escape of the\r
#Text=steam, and without it, the closing of the edge will not avail to keep\r
#Text=in the syrup.
#Text=It is a good way to invert a teacup in the centre of a\r
#Text=juicy fruit-pie, as in making an oyster-pie.
#Text=A clammy lower-crust is neither good or digestible.
#Text=Therefore never\r
#Text=fill pies made of moist materials until just before putting them into\r
#Text=the oven.
#Text=Squash pies, cocoanut, and Marlborough puddings, &c., should\r
#Text=not be filled until the last minute, and mince and stewed apple should\r
#Text=only stand long enough for the upper-crust to be laid on.
#Text=Pie-crust\r
#Text=becomes yellow from standing long before being baked; therefore, delay\r
#Text=rolling out the upper-crust for any kind of pies until the oven is\r
#Text=nearly ready.
#Text=Pastry should be baked in a quick oven, to be light, and\r
#Text=be slightly browned to be healthy.
#Text=When you bake pumpkin and similar\r
#Text=kinds of pies, if you have the least doubt whether the crust is well\r
#Text=done, set the dishes a few minutes on embers, or the top of a cooking\r
#Text=stove.
#Text=This sort of pies requires nearly an hour to bake; more, if the\r
#Text=dishes are very deep.
#Text=When done enough, the top will be gently swelled\r
#Text=all over, and in moving, tremble like jelly; if not done, the middle\r
#Text=will look like a thick liquid.
#Text=Most pies require an hour to bake; those\r
#Text=made of stewed apple or cranberry, three quarters of an hour.
#Text=Much\r
#Text=depends on the kind of oven used.
#Text=It is difficult to make flaky crust in warm weather.
#Text=But cooling the\r
#Text=butter and water with ice, and having the pastry-table in the cellar,\r
#Text=will insure tolerable success.
#Text=There is hardly another article of food in which so much is sacrificed\r
#Text=to appearance as in pastry.
#Text=Everybody likes a light crust, a little\r
#Text=brown, and not excessively rich, better than one that is half butter or\r
#Text=lard, and baked white.
#Text=Cherries should not be stewed or stoned for pies.
#Text=Apples, after they\r
#Text=are pared, cut, and cored, should be washed.
#Text=Steam pumpkin and squash,\r
#Text=or stew it with very little water.
#Text=Meat for pies must not be chopped\r
#Text=till after it is cold.
#Text=After a little practice and observation, it will be just as well to\r
#Text=omit weighing the materials for pastry.
#Text=One very heaping handful of\r
#Text=flour will make a common-sized pie; not, however, allowing for the\r
#Text=flour to be used in rolling the paste.
#Text=When all the pies but the last one are made, scrape the remains of\r
#Text=crust from the moulding-board and the rolling-pin, and add any parings\r
#Text=of edges that you have, work them together, and use for the under-crust.
#Text=For almost all kinds of pies, good brown sugar is nice enough.
#Text=The\r
#Text=Havana is seldom clean.
#Text=The Porto-Rico and Santa Cruz are considered\r
#Text=the best.
#Text=The New Orleans is very sweet.
#Text=The very early apples, when used for pies or sauce, should not be\r
#Text=pared, as the greatest part of the richness of the fruit, at that\r
#Text=season, is in the skin.
#Text=Some kinds are so delicate, that when stewed,\r
#Text=the skin is entirely absorbed in the pulp, so as not to be visible, and\r
#Text=the color, if it is red, is beautifully diffused through the whole mass.
#Text==Strawberries.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take large strawberries not extremely ripe; weigh equal quantities of\r
#Text=fruit and best sugar; lay the fruit in a dish, and sprinkle half the\r
#Text=sugar over it; shake the dish a little, that the sugar may touch all\r
#Text=the fruit.
#Text=Next day make a syrup of the remainder of the sugar and the\r
#Text=juice which you can pour off from the fruit in the pan, and as it boils\r
#Text=lay in the strawberries, and boil them gently twenty minutes or half an\r
#Text=hour.
#Text=DIRECTIONS FOR COOKING MEATS.
#Text==To Roast Beef.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=See the directions for roasting meat.
#Text==Beef Steak.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The best slices are cut from the rump, or through the sirloin.
#Text=The\r
#Text=round is seldom tender enough, and is very good cooked in other ways.
#Text=Do not cut your slices very thick.
#Text=Have the gridiron perfectly clean.
#Text=Set it over moderately hot coals at first, and turn the steaks in less\r
#Text=than a minute.
#Text=Turn them repeatedly.
#Text=If the fat makes a blaze under\r
#Text=the gridiron, put it out by sprinkling fine salt on it.
#Text=Steaks will\r
#Text=broil in about seven minutes.
#Text=Have ready a hot dish, and sprinkle each\r
#Text=piece with salt, and a little pepper; lay on small pieces of butter,\r
#Text=and cover close.
#Text=This is a much better way than to melt the butter in\r
#Text=the dish before taking up the meat.
#Text=Some persons keep a small pair of\r
#Text=tongs on purpose to turn beef-steaks, as using a fork wastes the juice.
#Text=Steaks should be served hot as possible.
#Text==Tomato Steak.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take two pounds of beef; cut it in small strips, and put it into\r
#Text=the pot with seven medium-sized tomatoes.
#Text=Stew it very slowly.
#Text=Add a\r
#Text=dessert spoonful of sugar, salt, a little clove, and, just before you\r
#Text=take it up, a dessert spoonful of butter.
#Text=If you have tomato catsup,\r
#Text=add a little, and if you like chopped onion, that also.
#Text=Very tender\r
#Text=beef is, of course, to be preferred; but that which is tough becomes\r
#Text=more palatable in this than in almost any other way.
#Text=This dish is quite\r
#Text=as good, if not better, heated over the next day.
#Text==Alamode Beef (in a plain way).=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a thick piece of flank, or, if most convenient, the thickest part\r
#Text=of the round, weighing six or eight pounds, for a small family of four\r
#Text=or five persons.
#Text=Cut off the strips of coarse fat upon the edge, make\r
#Text=incisions in all parts, and fill them with a stuffing made of bread,\r
#Text=salt pork chopped, pepper, and sweet marjoram.
#Text=Push whole cloves here\r
#Text=and there into the meat; roll it up, fasten it with skewers, and wind\r
#Text=a strong twine or tape about it.
#Text=Have ready a pot in which you have\r
#Text=fried to a crisp three or four slices of salt pork; take out the pork,\r
#Text=lay in the beef, and brown every side.
#Text=When well browned, add hardly\r
#Text=water enough to cover it, chop a large onion fine, add eighteen or\r
#Text=twenty cloves, and boil it gently, but steadily, three or four hours,\r
#Text=according to the size.
#Text=The water should boil away so as to make a rich\r
#Text=gravy, but be careful it does not burn.
#Text=When you take up the beef, add\r
#Text=browned flour to the gravy, if it needs to be thickened.
#Text==Stewed Brisket of Beef.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put three or four pounds of brisket into a kettle, and cover it with\r
#Text=water.
#Text=Take off the scum as it rises.
#Text=Let it boil steadily two hours.
#Text=Then take it from the pot and brown it with butter in a spider.
#Text=When it\r
#Text=is browned on every side, return it to the kettle, and stew it gently\r
#Text=five hours more.
#Text=Add more water if it boils away.
#Text=Put in a carrot and\r
#Text=a turnip or two, cut small, an onion also; a few cloves, and salt and\r
#Text=pepper as you think necessary.
#Text=Half an hour before dinner add tomato\r
#Text=or mushroom catsup.
#Text=To serve it, lay the beef upon a dish, and strew\r
#Text=capers over it.
#Text=The water in which it was stewed is a nice soup.
#Text==Stewed Tongue.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil a fresh tongue three hours, and if the skin does not easily come\r
#Text=off, boil it longer.
#Text=Remove the skin; strain the water in which it\r
#Text=was boiled.
#Text=Wash the pot, and return the tongue to it, with enough of\r
#Text=the strained liquor to cover it.
#Text=Put in it a carrot, a turnip, and an\r
#Text=onion, cut fine, and a table-spoonful of powdered clove and also of\r
#Text=ground pepper, tied up in muslin bags.
#Text=Boil the tongue gently two hours\r
#Text=and a half.
#Text=About fifteen minutes before it is taken up, toast two\r
#Text=slices of bread without the crust, cut it up in small bits, and put it\r
#Text=into the pot.
#Text=When you dish it up, put about a pint of the liquor and\r
#Text=vegetables round the tongue in a fricassee dish.
#Text=DIRECTIONS FOR SALTING MEAT, FISH, &c.
#Text=To some young housekeepers, the salting of meat, and taking care\r
#Text=of it, and of smoked meat, are perplexing.
#Text=Perhaps the following\r
#Text=directions may assist them.
#Text=The best pieces to corn are the end of the\r
#Text=rump, the thin end of the sirloin, and the edge-bone.
#Text=If you like it\r
#Text=with alternate streaks of fat and lean, the pieces at the ends of the\r
#Text=ribs, called by butchers the rattle-ran, are very good.
#Text=The edge-bone\r
#Text=affords the most lean meat.
#Text=The best piece of pork to corn is the shoulder.
#Text=It is a good way to\r
#Text=divide it, if large, and stuff half of it with sage and bread crumbs,\r
#Text=and roast it; and corn the other half.
#Text=In winter, hang fresh killed meat up two or three days before putting\r
#Text=it into brine, as it will thus become more tender.
#Text=Make a brine of\r
#Text=four quarts of water, three pints of salt, half a table-spoonful of\r
#Text=saltpetre, and a pint of molasses, or a pound of coarse brown sugar.
#Text=Mix it thoroughly without boiling it.
#Text=In this lay the meat, and see\r
#Text=that it is entirely covered.
#Text=It is well to look at it after a day or\r
#Text=two, and if necessary, turn it the other side up.
#Text=It will be good in\r
#Text=a few days, but it is better to let it lie three or four weeks before\r
#Text=boiling it.
#Text=The same brine will do for many successive pieces in\r
#Text=winter.
#Text=But for a family that like salt meat, it is the best way to\r
#Text=make a double measure, and put into it at once as much meat as it will\r
#Text=cover.
#Text=It should be kept in a firkin or tub, with a close cover.
#Text=After a considerable quantity of meat has thus been cured, scald and\r
#Text=skim the brine, add a little more molasses, salt, and saltpetre, and\r
#Text=let it become cold before meat is put into it.
#Text=A brine like this, only a little more rich with molasses, is very good\r
#Text=for salting tongues, and pieces that are to be smoked.
#Text=But they should\r
#Text=lie in it four or five weeks.
#Text=Meat should never be salted for smoking,\r
#Text=later than February or the middle of March.
#Text=In warm weather, it will not do to use the same brine more than once,\r
#Text=as the blood from the meat will become tainted.
#Text=Therefore a less\r
#Text=expensive mixture, that may be thrown away after being used once, is\r
#Text=better.
#Text=Two quarts of salt to four of water, is a good rule for brine\r
#Text=in hot weather.
#Text=In the summer, the strong membrane that covers the rib bones, must be\r
#Text=cut open with a sharp knife before the meat is put into brine; for, as\r
#Text=the salt will not penetrate this membrane, the bones will else become\r
#Text=tainted, and the meat soon be spoiled.
#Text=Meat, at this season, should be\r
#Text=cooked within three or four days after being put into brine.
#Text==To Salt Pork.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Allow a bushel of salt for a barrel of pork, or a peck for fifty\r
#Text=weight.
#Text=The salt called _coarse-fine_, is commonly used by butchers;\r
#Text=but the best way in a private family, where no more than twenty-five or\r
#Text=fifty weight is put down for the year's use, is to use fine salt.
#Text=Put\r
#Text=water enough to cover it.
#Text=Examine it in a few days, and if the salt is\r
#Text=all dissolved, add more.
#Text=The only sure way of keeping pork sweet, is to\r
#Text=have the brine so strong that some of the salt remains undissolved.
#Text=A\r
#Text=board, with a stone upon it, should always be kept on the top of pork,\r
#Text=as it will soon become rusty if the edges lie above the surface of the\r
#Text=brine.
#Text=It is not fit for use, until it has been in brine six weeks.
#Text==Pickle for one Ham.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To a gallon of water, put a pint of salt, a pint of molasses, and an\r
#Text=ounce of saltpetre.
#Text=Turn the ham over in the brine often, and let it\r
#Text=lie in it six weeks; then let it be smoked nearly as long.
#Text==To Cure Hams.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[This receipt is furnished by a person whose hams are celebrated in the\r
#Text=eastern part of Massachusetts, for their superior quality.]
#Text=For curing fifty weight, allow three quarts of coarse salt, half a\r
#Text=pound of saltpetre, and two quarts of good molasses.
#Text=Add soft water\r
#Text=enough just to cover the hams.
#Text=Common sized hams should be kept in this\r
#Text=pickle five weeks; larger ones six.
#Text=They should all be taken out once a\r
#Text=week, and those which were on the top laid in first, and the lower ones\r
#Text=last.
#Text=They should be smoked from two to three weeks with walnut wood\r
#Text=or with sawdust and corn-cobs, mixed.
#Text=Meat smoked with cobs is very\r
#Text=delicate.
#Text=Pieces of beef for smoking, may be laid in this pickle, after the hams\r
#Text=are sent to the smoke house; but more salt should be added.
#Text==The Knickerbocker Pickle.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To three gallons of soft water, put four pounds and a half of salt,\r
#Text=coarse and fine, mixed; a pound and a half of brown sugar, an ounce and\r
#Text=a half of saltpetre, half an ounce of saleratus, and two quarts of good\r
#Text=molasses.
#Text=Boil the mixture, skim it well, and when cold pour it over the hams or\r
#Text=beef.
#Text=Beef laid down in this pickle, does not become hard, and is very\r
#Text=fine, when boiled gently and long.
#Text=Some persons consider this the best of all methods for curing beef and\r
#Text=hams.
#Text==How to keep Hams through the Summer.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=When they are taken from the smoke house, do not suffer them to lie\r
#Text=a single hour where the flies can find them.
#Text=Sew them up in a coarse\r
#Text=cloth or stiff brown paper, and pack them in ashes.
#Text=There is no method\r
#Text=so sure to preserve them from insects, and the effect of the ashes\r
#Text=is to improve the meat; but care should be taken that the hams are\r
#Text=so secured that the ashes will not touch them.
#Text=The ashes should be\r
#Text=perfectly cold and dry, and the barrel be in a dry, cool place.
#Text==To make Sausages.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=A common fault is, that the meat is not chopped enough.
#Text=It should be\r
#Text=chopped very fine, and this is most easily done if it is a little\r
#Text=frozen.
#Text=When ready for the seasoning, put in just cold water enough to\r
#Text=enable you to mix the ingredients equally; but be careful not to use\r
#Text=more than is necessary for this purpose.
#Text=The following excellent rule for seasoning sausages is furnished by\r
#Text=the same person whose receipt for curing hams I have been allowed to\r
#Text=copy.
#Text=To twelve pounds and a half of meat put a gill of fine salt, a large\r
#Text=gill of powdered sage, and half a gill of ground pepper.
#Text=Let the\r
#Text=measures be exact.
#Text=Some persons find it most convenient to keep sausage meat in a cloth.
#Text=It is done by making a long bag of strong cotton cloth, of such a size\r
#Text=that, when filled, it will be as large round as a common half pint mug.
#Text=It should be crowded full, and each end tied up.
#Text=If you have not a\r
#Text=sausage-filler, it can be filled with the hand.
#Text=Sew up only a quarter\r
#Text=of a yard, then fill it tight, so far; then sew another quarter, and\r
#Text=fill it, and so on until you reach the end.
#Text=When the meat is to be\r
#Text=used, open one end, rip up the seam a little way, and cut off slices\r
#Text=rather more than an inch thick, and fry them.
#Text=It may be kept good from\r
#Text=December to March, in a cold, dry place.
#Text==How to salt Shad to keep a Year.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Procure those which are just caught; soak them an hour or two in a\r
#Text=plenty of water, in order that the scales may be easily taken off.
#Text=Take\r
#Text=care to remove them all.
#Text=Cut off the heads and open them down the back.
#Text=When you have taken out all the refuse parts, remove the greatest part\r
#Text=of the spine, as the fish will be more sure to keep sweet.
#Text=A sharp\r
#Text=knife is indispensable.
#Text=Lay them in fresh water with a good deal of\r
#Text=salt in it for an hour or two, in order to extract the blood.
#Text=Then\r
#Text=take them out, and sprinkle them plentifully with fine salt, taking\r
#Text=care that it touches all the ends and edges.
#Text=If most convenient, let\r
#Text=them lie over night.
#Text=In the morning, mingle an ounce of saltpetre and\r
#Text=a pound of sugar with a peck of _coarse-fine_ salt, and put a layer of\r
#Text=salt, and a layer of fish (the skin being down), into the firkin.
#Text=A\r
#Text=peck of salt will cure twenty-five shad.
#Text==To try Lard.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The fat should not be suffered to stand long without being _tried_,\r
#Text=because, even in cold weather, some parts of it may soon become musty,\r
#Text=and nothing can then restore its sweetness.
#Text=Remove all the lean bits,\r
#Text=as they will adhere to the kettle, and cause the fat to burn.
#Text=Cut\r
#Text=it into pieces a little more than an inch square, and take care to\r
#Text=have them nearly of a size.
#Text=Put a little water into the kettle, and\r
#Text=keep a steady, good fire, without much blaze, and stir the fat often.
#Text=Attention to the kettle and the fire will be necessary, through the\r
#Text=process.
#Text=It will require three hours to do it.
#Text=When the fat no longer\r
#Text=bubbles, but is still, it is done enough.
#Text=It is best to squeeze it\r
#Text=through a tow cloth bag, made by folding half a square in such a way\r
#Text=that the corner will form the end, and it should be rounded off a\r
#Text=little at the bottom, and the seam made exactly as directed for a\r
#Text=pudding-bag.
#Text=Two pieces of wood fastened together, somewhat like a\r
#Text=lemon-squeezer, will facilitate the process of straining it.
#Text=Strain\r
#Text=all that flows off without much pressure into one jar, and that which\r
#Text=is extracted last, into another.
#Text=There is no advantage in putting salt\r
#Text=into lard.
#Text=It does not mingle with it, as appears by its being always\r
#Text=found at the bottom of the kettle, undissolved.
#Text=Stone jars are best for\r
#Text=keeping lard, but potter's ware does very well.
#Text=It should stand in a\r
#Text=cold place, and in warm weather, a fire-place with a close board, in a\r
#Text=cool room, is a very good place to keep it.
#Text=Scraps are a favorite dish with many persons.
#Text=Put salt, pepper, and\r
#Text=pulverized sage to them, while they are still warm, break them small,\r
#Text=and stir them well that the seasoning may be equally distributed.
#Text==Preserved.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Having skinned them, weigh equal quantities of fruit and sugar.
#Text=Let the\r
#Text=tomatoes lie upon a hair sieve a little while in order that some of the\r
#Text=juice may drain out.
#Text=Then lay them carefully, so as not to spoil the\r
#Text=shape, into a stone jar, in alternate layers with the sugar.
#Text=Allow one\r
#Text=lemon for every four pounds of fruit, and lay slices of lemon between\r
#Text=each layer of fruit.
#Text=Cover the jar close, and set it in a kettle\r
#Text=of cold water, where it will boil moderately, but constantly, many\r
#Text=hours--all day if possible.
#Text=See that the water comes up high enough\r
#Text=around the jar, and also that none of it boils into the top.
#Text=When it\r
#Text=is boiled enough, let the jar stand until the water has in a measure\r
#Text=cooled, as it may be broken by being taken at once out of boiling water.
#Text==Figs.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Choose smooth-shaped tomatoes, and to sixteen pounds allow six pounds\r
#Text=of sugar.
#Text=Scald and remove the skins in the usual way; put the sugar\r
#Text=to them, and boil until penetrated with it; then take them out, spread\r
#Text=them on dishes, flatten and dry them in the sun.
#Text=A small quantity of\r
#Text=sugar should be sprinkled over them occasionally while drying.
#Text=When\r
#Text=perfectly dry, pack them in boxes, sprinkling each layer with powdered\r
#Text=sugar.
#Text==Pickle= (an excellent Condiment).
#Text=Put eight pounds of skinned tomatoes, and four of brown sugar, into a\r
#Text=preserving kettle.
#Text=Stir often and see they do not burn.
#Text=Boil them to\r
#Text=the consistency of molasses, then add a quart of sharp cider-vinegar,\r
#Text=a teaspoonful of mace, another of cinnamon, and half a teaspoonful of\r
#Text=clove, and boil five minutes longer.
#Text==Stewed Tomato= (to keep the year round).
#Text=Skin and cut up the fruit, and boil it gently two hours in a porcelain\r
#Text=kettle; add nothing to it but a little salt.
#Text=Have ready enough clean\r
#Text=bottles to contain the quantity to be stewed.
#Text=Olive bottles are very\r
#Text=convenient for the purpose, but common junk bottles are also good.
#Text=Provide a tunnel, good corks, a coarse towel, a hammer, and a tin dish\r
#Text=containing equal parts of rosin and shoemaker's wax.
#Text=After two hours'\r
#Text=boiling, set the kettle off; have the bottles ready warmed by standing\r
#Text=near the fire so that heat will not crack them, put hot water into\r
#Text=three or four at a time, shake it about, and drain it out; then fill\r
#Text=the bottles with the hot tomato _nearly_ far enough to meet the cork.
#Text=If it does not readily go through the tunnel, push it down with a stick\r
#Text=or skewer.
#Text=When you have filled these, put in the corks and hammer\r
#Text=them down; take the coarse towel to protect your hands from the heat,\r
#Text=and dip the mouth of the bottle into the melted sealing-wax.
#Text=See that\r
#Text=the cork is entirely covered by it.
#Text=Set these aside and do the rest in\r
#Text=the same way.
#Text=This is a convenient way for those who do not own the\r
#Text=cans now so much used; and tomatoes put up thus, are as good months\r
#Text=afterwards as if the fruit was just gathered.
#Text=None but fresh and sound\r
#Text=ones should be used.
#Text=Set the bottles in a cool, dry place.
#Text==Pickled.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash green tomatoes, and slice them rather thin; weigh them, and allow\r
#Text=three or four sliced onions, four pounds of sugar, and a gallon of\r
#Text=cider-vinegar to eight pounds of tomatoes.
#Text=Put the vinegar to boil in\r
#Text=a porcelain kettle with the sugar, stir it, and when it boils up, set\r
#Text=it off, and let it stand a few minutes until you can remove the scum\r
#Text=without wasting the vinegar; then add the onions, two teaspoonfuls\r
#Text=of salt, a table-spoonful each of powdered cinnamon and clove, and\r
#Text=a grated nutmeg; then set it upon the fire and immediately add the\r
#Text=tomatoes.
#Text=When the vinegar begins to simmer press the tomatoes gently\r
#Text=down.
#Text=Let them boil only two or three minutes.
#Text=Put them into covered\r
#Text=jars; or, when cool enough into wide-mouthed bottles.
#Text=When the pickles\r
#Text=are all used, the vinegar need not be lost, as it is excellent upon\r
#Text=baked beans, and cold salt meat, or in mince pies.
#Text=THE CARE OF MILK, AND MAKING BUTTER.
#Text=No branch of household economy brings a better reward than the making\r
#Text=of butter; and to one who takes an interest in domestic employments, it\r
#Text=soon becomes a pleasant occupation.
#Text=The following instructions are derived from the personal experience of\r
#Text=one of the most skilful dairy-women in New England; and by observing\r
#Text=them, the youthful house-keeper, hitherto unpractised in such\r
#Text=mysteries, will have the pleasure of furnishing her table with the\r
#Text=finest butter, the work of her own hands.
#Text=The first requisite is to have a good cow.
#Text=One that has high hips,\r
#Text=short fore-legs and a large udder is to be preferred.
#Text=The cream-colored\r
#Text=and the mouse-colored cows generally give a large quantity and of rich\r
#Text=quality.
#Text=Her feeding should be faithfully attended to.
#Text=She should have\r
#Text=a good pasture not far distant, or if this is impracticable, care must\r
#Text=be taken that she is not made to run--a piece of mischief frequently\r
#Text=practised.
#Text=Give her a teacupful of salt once a week.
#Text=Feed her once\r
#Text=a day with the waste from the kitchen, adding to it about a pint of\r
#Text=Indian meal.
#Text=Give her the skimmed milk not wanted in the family.
#Text=If\r
#Text=she does not readily drink it, teach her by keeping her a few days\r
#Text=without an ample supply of water.
#Text=Take care that nothing is given her\r
#Text=which will injure the taste of the milk, such as turnips and parsnips.
#Text=Carrots are a fine vegetable for cows.
#Text=Have her milked by a person\r
#Text=who understands the process, or she will not give it freely, and will\r
#Text=soon become dry.
#Text=But the most abundant supply of the richest milk will\r
#Text=avail little, unless all the articles used in the care of it are kept\r
#Text=in perfect order.
#Text=They should not be used for other purposes.
#Text=Keep a\r
#Text=cloth for washing them only, and never wash them in the same water with\r
#Text=other dishes.
#Text=After washing, every article, and the cloth with which\r
#Text=they are washed, must be scalded.
#Text=Wash off thoroughly all the milk from\r
#Text=the pans, pail, strainer, churn, dasher, skimmer, spoons, &c., before\r
#Text=scalding them.
#Text=If milk remains in them when scalded, the butter will be\r
#Text=injured, as may be supposed, from the fact that a cloth strainer, if\r
#Text=scalded a few times with milk in it, becomes yellow, and as stiff as if\r
#Text=it were starched.
#Text=To scald them the water must actually boil.
#Text=Have a kettle of a size to\r
#Text=admit the pail and pans, and plunge all the articles into it; as, if\r
#Text=the water is only poured on, the edges of the pan and the ears of the\r
#Text=pail will not always be well scalded.
#Text=If a cloth strainer is used, it should be of thin, coarse linen.
#Text=A\r
#Text=basin having a fine wire strainer is used by many persons.
#Text=Tin pails\r
#Text=and pans are better than wood and earthen; because tin is more easily\r
#Text=kept sweet than wood, and the glazing upon brown earthen pans is\r
#Text=sometimes decomposed by sour milk.[17] Large wooden churns, worked by\r
#Text=dogs trained to the business, are used in large dairies; but those who\r
#Text=keep one or two cows only, will find a stone-ware churn best.
#Text=No other\r
#Text=is so easily kept sweet.
#Text=For keeping the cream, never use tin, but\r
#Text=always stone, cream-colored or fire-proof ware.
#Text=For working butter,\r
#Text=keep a wooden bowl and ladle.
#Text=This last article is seldom found in New\r
#Text=England, but always in the State of New York.
#Text=Every butter-maker should\r
#Text=have it, as the warmth of the hand detracts from the sweetness of the\r
#Text=butter
#Text=.
#Text=[17] About two years since four men, while making hay in a warm day,\r
#Text= drank buttermilk which had been kept in a jar of potter's ware, and\r
#Text= every one died immediately.
#Text=Have the milk closet on the coolest side of the house, or in the\r
#Text=dryest and coolest part of the cellar, and with a window in it covered\r
#Text=with wire-net or slats.
#Text=Good butter cannot be made without a free\r
#Text=circulation of fresh air.
#Text=Allow no drops of cream or milk to remain a\r
#Text=day on the shelves.
#Text=Every inch of such a closet must be kept perfectly\r
#Text=clean.
#Text=Strain the milk as soon as it is brought in, and set it immediately in\r
#Text=its place.
#Text=To remove milk after the cream has begun to rise, prevents\r
#Text=its rising freely.
#Text=For the same reason the smallest quantity should not\r
#Text=be taken from a pan set for raising cream; therefore all that is wanted\r
#Text=for the day's use, must be set apart from the other pans.
#Text=Those who\r
#Text=have ice through the summer, have a valuable aid in making good butter.
#Text=A piece as large as a peach, should be put into a pan containing three\r
#Text=quarts of milk, as soon as it is placed in the closet.
#Text=The milk will\r
#Text=not sour as soon, and of course will afford more cream.
#Text=Skim the cream\r
#Text=as soon as the milk has become _loppord_, which will, in hot weather,\r
#Text=be in about thirty hours.
#Text=To do this, first pass the fore-finger round\r
#Text=the edge of the pan; (this is better than to use the skimmer, because\r
#Text=there is a hard, wiry edge of cream adhering to the pan, which if taken\r
#Text=off will injure the butter;) then take off the cream, clear as possible\r
#Text=from the milk.
#Text=In very hot weather, especially in August, which is the least favorable\r
#Text=month for making butter, a heaping spoonful of salt should be put into\r
#Text=a pailful of milk, after the portion for the ordinary family uses is\r
#Text=taken out; and at all seasons, fine salt should be put into the cream\r
#Text=from day to day, as it is gathered.
#Text=The effect of this is excellent, in\r
#Text=keeping it sweet and giving a rich flavor to the butter.
#Text=The finest butter is made where the number of cows renders it necessary\r
#Text=to churn every day.
#Text=The custom of churning once a week is not to be\r
#Text=tolerated.
#Text=Cream that is kept seven days, unless it be in the coldest\r
#Text=weather, cannot be made into good butter.
#Text=If you keep but one cow,\r
#Text=churn twice a week; and in dog-days, three times.
#Text=Do it in the cool of\r
#Text=the morning.
#Text=If the weather is warm, set the churn into a tub of cold\r
#Text=water; add ice if you have it, and put a piece also into the churn.
#Text=Air\r
#Text=is necessary to make butter _come_; therefore, if the cream flies out\r
#Text=of the opening around the dasher, do not put any thing round to prevent\r
#Text=it.
#Text=When the butter has come, continue the strokes of the dasher a few\r
#Text=minutes to separate all the little particles from the butter-milk.
#Text=This\r
#Text=done, take it out into the wooden bowl with a ladle or skimmer.
#Text=The\r
#Text=bowl and ladle should have boiling water poured on them when you first\r
#Text=begin to churn.
#Text=After a few minutes it should be poured off, and cold\r
#Text=water be poured on them, and they should stand till you are ready to\r
#Text=use them.
#Text=This is to prevent the butter from sticking to them.
#Text=Work the butter with the ladle, until the buttermilk ceases to come\r
#Text=out; then sprinkle it with clean sifted salt, as that which was put\r
#Text=into the cream will not be enough; work it in well, and taste it to see\r
#Text=if more should be added.
#Text=Observation and experience must teach you how\r
#Text=much to use.
#Text=Mould the butter with the ladle into balls or lumps of any\r
#Text=form you prefer; put it into a covered jar or tureen and set it in the\r
#Text=ice-house or cellar.
#Text=Butter is sweetest to be worked but once, and if all which you make is\r
#Text=used from week to week, it is sufficient, provided it comes hard; if\r
#Text=it is soft at first, it must be worked again the next morning.
#Text=That\r
#Text=which is to be laid down for future use, or to be kept two or three\r
#Text=weeks, must be worked again after a day or two, and every particle of\r
#Text=buttermilk got out.
#Text=Never work butter a third time.
#Text=From October to June, the best method of raising cream is to set the\r
#Text=pans for twelve hours in the milk closet, and then for five hours on\r
#Text=a stove, or a furnace having embers in it, where the milk will become\r
#Text=hot, _but not scald_; then return it to the closet, and after it is\r
#Text=cold, take off the cream, draining it very clear from the milk.
#Text=Much\r
#Text=more cream will be obtained in this, than in the ordinary method; and\r
#Text=at least a quarter more butter will be secured from the same quantity\r
#Text=of milk.
#Text=It also comes very quick--ten minutes' churning being often\r
#Text=sufficient.
#Text=This is the method practised in Devonshire, England; and\r
#Text=the _clotted cream_, as it is there called, is carried up to the London\r
#Text=market; for it is not only good for butter, but also for coffee and\r
#Text=other uses.
#Text=Care must be taken that the milk is not made too hot.
#Text=If it\r
#Text=becomes so hot as almost to scald, the cream will have little skinny\r
#Text=flakes in it, which will be visible in the butter.
#Text==A good Brine for keeping Butter.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To two quarts of water, put one of clean fine salt, a pound of loaf\r
#Text=or crushed sugar, and a teaspoonful of saltpetre.
#Text=When it has stood an\r
#Text=hour, in order that the salt and sugar may dissolve, strain it through\r
#Text=a flannel bag, and pour it over the butter.
#Text=Less salt may be enough.
#Text=The object is to have as much as the water will take up.
#Text==To keep Butter sweet a Year.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take care that the butter is made in the best manner, and the\r
#Text=buttermilk entirely worked out of it.
#Text=Lay it in a white-oak firkin.
#Text=Make a strong brine of salt and water, and put it into another and\r
#Text=larger firkin, and set the one containing the butter into the one in\r
#Text=which the brine is.
#Text=Let the brine come up very near to the top of the\r
#Text=butter firkin.
#Text=Lay on the top of the butter a white bag with fine salt\r
#Text=in it, cover it close, and then put on the cover of the outside firkin.
#Text=FOOD AND DRINKS FOR THE SICK, AND FOR INFANTS.
#Text==Beef Tea.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut a piece of lean, juicy beef into pieces an inch square, put them\r
#Text=into a wide-mouthed bottle and cork it tight.
#Text=Set the bottle into a\r
#Text=kettle of cold water and boil it an hour and a half.
#Text=This mode of\r
#Text=making beef tea concentrates the nourishment more than any other.
#Text==Another= (furnished by a physician).
#Text=Take a piece of beef cut from the round; take off every particle of\r
#Text=fat, then cut it into pieces about an inch square and put into cold\r
#Text=water, in the proportion of a pint to the pound.
#Text=After standing half\r
#Text=or three quarters of an hour, set it on the fire and boil it slowly\r
#Text=several hours.
#Text=If the water boils away, add more cold water, so that\r
#Text=there will be a pint of tea for every pound of beef.
#Text=Strain it, add\r
#Text=salt, and black pepper also if the case allows it.
#Text==Another Way.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Choose a lean and juicy piece of beef, the size of your hand; take off\r
#Text=all the fat; broil it only three or four minutes, on very hot coals.
#Text=Lay it in a porringer or bowl, sprinkle it with salt, and pour upon it\r
#Text=two or three gills of boiling water; then cut it into small pieces, as\r
#Text=it lies in the water.
#Text=Cover it close, and let it stand where it will\r
#Text=keep hot but not boil.
#Text=It is fit for use in half an hour, and does well\r
#Text=where such nourishment is wanted immediately.
#Text=This is more agreeable to the taste than tea made by either of the two\r
#Text=preceding rules, but it is not as good for a patient who is so sick as\r
#Text=to take but very little nourishment at once.
#Text==Chicken Broth.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=If the weather is warm, use but half a chicken to make broth for one\r
#Text=person.
#Text=If it is cool take a whole one, as the broth will keep several\r
#Text=days.
#Text=Pull off the skin (because there is a good deal of oil in it)\r
#Text=and allow two quarts of water for a chicken.
#Text=Skim it in the neatest\r
#Text=manner when it begins to boil.
#Text=Put in a large spoonful of rice, and a\r
#Text=teaspoonful of salt, and boil it slowly two hours.
#Text=If onion and parsley\r
#Text=are to be added, cut them fine; put in the onion when the broth has\r
#Text=boiled an hour, and the parsley five minutes before it is served.
#Text=It is the best way to boil the chicken the day before it is wanted, and\r
#Text=the next day take off the fat, add the rice, &c., and boil it another\r
#Text=hour.
#Text==Chicken Tea.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a leg and thigh of a chicken, lay it into a pint of cold water,\r
#Text=and set it on the fire till it boils up long enough for you to skim it.
#Text=Put in a little salt.
#Text==Chicken Panada.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil a young chicken half an hour in a quart of water.
#Text=Then remove the\r
#Text=skin, cut off the white meat, and when cold, put it into a mortar with\r
#Text=a spoonful or two of the water in which it was boiled, and pound it to\r
#Text=a paste.
#Text=Season it with salt, and a very little nutmeg; add a little\r
#Text=more of the water, and boil it up three or four minutes.
#Text=It should be\r
#Text=of such a consistency that it can be drank, though rather thick.
#Text=The bones which remain may be returned to the water in which the\r
#Text=chicken was boiled; and with the addition of rice, a good broth be made\r
#Text=of it.
#Text==Calf's foot Broth.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil two feet in three quarts of water, until it is wasted to three\r
#Text=pints.
#Text=Strain it, and set it aside in a cool place.
#Text=When cold, take off\r
#Text=the fat.
#Text=Heat a little at a time as it is wanted, and add salt, nutmeg,\r
#Text=and, if approved, a spoonful of good wine.
#Text==Wine Whey.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To a pint of milk put two glasses of wine; mix it, and let it stand\r
#Text=twelve minutes, then strain it through a muslin bag or a very fine\r
#Text=sieve.
#Text=Sweeten it with loaf sugar.
#Text=If it is necessary to have the whey weaker, put a little hot water to\r
#Text=the milk.
#Text==Barley Water.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil an ounce of pearl barley a few minutes to cleanse it, pour off the\r
#Text=water, and put a quart of cold water and a little salt to it.
#Text=Simmer it\r
#Text=an hour.
#Text==Arrow-root.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The best kinds of arrow-root are the Jamaica and Bermuda.
#Text=Wet a large teaspoonful in a little cold water, with half a teaspoonful\r
#Text=of salt; pour on it half a pint of boiling water, stirring it very\r
#Text=fast.
#Text=Then set it where it will just boil up for one minute.
#Text=Sweeten\r
#Text=it, and add milk if it is allowed.
#Text=For a drink, make it very thin, and\r
#Text=put in lemon juice and sugar.
#Text==Pearl Sago, and Tapioca.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The directions, page 82 are appropriate for the preparation of these\r
#Text=articles for invalids.
#Text==Milk Porridge.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put to half a pint of boiling water, two teaspoonfuls of flour wet\r
#Text=smooth in cold water, and add salt.
#Text=Then put in half a pint of milk,\r
#Text=stir it well, and let it boil up again.
#Text=Vary the proportions of milk\r
#Text=and water as the case requires.
#Text=Made wholly with milk it is a very\r
#Text=hearty dish.
#Text==Oatmeal Gruel.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put two large spoonfuls of oatmeal, wet in cold water, into three pints\r
#Text=of boiling water; boil it gently half an hour, skim it, add a little\r
#Text=salt, sugar, and nutmeg.
#Text=If raisins are also used, a large teacupful\r
#Text=stoned, will be enough.
#Text=But gruel with raisins should be boiled longer\r
#Text=than without.
#Text==Ground Rice Gruel.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Rub a heaping teaspoonful of ground rice in a small quantity of cold\r
#Text=water, and stir it into half a pint of boiling water; add a little\r
#Text=salt, and let it boil up half a minute.
#Text=If milk is allowed, it is an\r
#Text=improvement to make the gruel with equal parts of milk and water.
#Text==Indian Meal Gruel.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=This is made in the same way as the ground rice, but requires much\r
#Text=longer boiling.
#Text=It should never be boiled less than half an hour, and\r
#Text=an hour is much better.
#Text=The white froth that rises upon the top should\r
#Text=never be skimmed off, as it is the most nutritious part of the gruel.
#Text=Nutmeg, sugar, and a spoonful of cream may be added, if approved.
#Text==Panada.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Set a saucepan with three gills of water upon the fire, add one glass\r
#Text=of white wine, a little loaf sugar, and a very little nutmeg, and\r
#Text=grated lemon.
#Text=Meanwhile, grate some white bread, and the moment the\r
#Text=mixture boils, put in the bread, keeping it still on the fire.
#Text=Let it\r
#Text=boil fast, and when of a thickness just to allow of drinking it, set it\r
#Text=off.
#Text==A Nutritious Jelly.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take of rice, sago, pearl barley, and hartshorn shavings, each an\r
#Text=ounce; add three pints of water, simmer it till reduced to one, and\r
#Text=then strain it.
#Text=When cold, it will be a jelly, to be given dissolved in\r
#Text=broth, milk, or wine, as directed by the physician.
#Text==Caudle.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Into a pint of thin rice gruel put, while it is boiling hot, a mixture\r
#Text=made of the yolk of an egg, beaten well with sugar, a large spoonful of\r
#Text=cold water, a glass of wine, and some nutmeg.
#Text=It should be stirred in\r
#Text=by degrees.
#Text==Rennet Whey.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash a piece of rennet an inch or two square, and lay it into half a\r
#Text=gill of warm water for an hour.
#Text=Warm a pint of milk, but do not make it\r
#Text=hot; put it into a shallow dish, and stir the rennet-water into it.
#Text=Let\r
#Text=it stand undisturbed half an hour, then cut it across many times with\r
#Text=a knife, and after an hour pour off the whey.
#Text=Let the dish then remain\r
#Text=several hours undisturbed, and more whey will be formed.
#Text=In cases of great debility of the stomach, consequent upon\r
#Text=inflammation, or attended with it, rennet whey will be retained when\r
#Text=every thing else is rejected, and may be given, a teaspoonful at the\r
#Text=time, very often, in order to prepare the stomach to receive and retain\r
#Text=nourishment.
#Text==Apple Tea.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Roast sour apples and pour boiling water upon them.
#Text=Let them stand till\r
#Text=the water is cold.
#Text==Another.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Pare and slice thin three or four pleasant sour apples, pour a pint\r
#Text=of boiling water on them, and boil them six or eight minutes.
#Text=Let\r
#Text=them stand till they are cold, then pour or strain off the water, and\r
#Text=sweeten it a little, unless the invalid prefers it without.
#Text=It is a\r
#Text=refreshing drink.
#Text==A Refreshing Draught in a Fever.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash a few sprigs of sage, burnet, balm, and sorrel, and put them into\r
#Text=a jug with half a sliced lemon.
#Text=Pour in three pints of boiling water,\r
#Text=sweeten it, and stop it close.
#Text==Crust Coffee.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a large crust of bread; brown is to be preferred, but Graham bread\r
#Text=will answer.
#Text=Dry it in the toaster, and at last almost burn both sides;\r
#Text=lay it in a saucepan and pour boiling water on it; boil it up a minute\r
#Text=or two, and then strain off the coffee; return it to the saucepan with\r
#Text=a little milk or cream, and boil it up again.
#Text=It should be made strong\r
#Text=enough to look like real coffee, of which it is a very good imitation\r
#Text=when well made.
#Text==Toast Water.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Toast a crust of white bread very brown without burning it, and put it\r
#Text=into cold water.
#Text=After an hour, the water will be a refreshing drink;\r
#Text=and it is sometimes grateful to the stomach when no other can be taken.
#Text==Herb Drinks.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Herb drinks should be made with boiling water in an earthen pitcher or\r
#Text=tea-pot, and be drank after standing a few minutes without boiling.
#Text=Long steeping makes them insipid and disagreeable.
#Text=All food and drink for the sick should be prepared with careful\r
#Text=attention and perfect neatness, and should be served in as inviting a\r
#Text=manner as possible.
#Text=The appetite of an invalid is excited or checked by\r
#Text=things that escape the observation of a person in health.
#Text==Food for a Young Infant.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Pour four spoonfuls of boiling water upon one of sweet cream, and add\r
#Text=a very little loaf sugar.
#Text=This receipt was given by an experienced\r
#Text=physician, and has been proved, to be entirely suited to the stomach of\r
#Text=the youngest infant.
#Text=But care must be taken to secure _good_ cream; and\r
#Text=this can be done only by providing new milk every day, from _one_ cow.
#Text=Mixed milk cannot be safely used for a little infant.
#Text==For a child just weaned.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=There is always danger, especially in warm weather, that the stomach,\r
#Text=even of a healthy child, will become disordered by being weaned; and\r
#Text=it is important to guard against the evil, by careful attention to the\r
#Text=diet, for a little while.
#Text=Boil every morning new milk enough to last\r
#Text=twenty-four hours, and stir into it the best of arrow-root wet in cold\r
#Text=water, in the proportion of a large teaspoonful to a quart.
#Text=Add a very\r
#Text=little salt, and boil it up again for one minute, then set it in a cold\r
#Text=place.
#Text==Flour Gruel= (for children sick with teething complaints).
#Text=Tie up in a piece of thick cotton cloth a coffee-cup of white flour.
#Text=Put it into boiling water, and keep it boiling steadily three hours.
#Text=Then remove the cloth and lay the lump where it will become perfectly\r
#Text=dry.
#Text=To use it, grate it and thicken two gills of boiling milk with\r
#Text=a dessert spoonful of it wet in cold water.
#Text=Put a little salt in the\r
#Text=milk.
#Text=This is excellent food for feeble children.
#Text=[The value of the following receipts has been proved in the successful\r
#Text=rearing of very feeble infants by the use of them.
#Text=Several mothers\r
#Text=have gratefully testified to their excellence, especially for children\r
#Text=reduced to extreme debility by teething complaints.
#Text=After weighing the\r
#Text=articles a few times it will be easy to proportion the ingredients by\r
#Text=measure].
#Text==Food for an Infant at successive periods.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=_For the first three months_:--5 grains of gelatine; 25 grains of\r
#Text=arrow-root; 2 gills of milk; 1 gill of cream; 1½ pints of water.
#Text=_From three to six months_:--gelatine, arrow-root, and water, as above;\r
#Text=3 gills of milk; 1 gill of cream.
#Text=_From six to nine months_:--gelatine, arrow-root, and water, as above;\r
#Text=1 pint of milk; 1½ gills of cream.
#Text=_From nine to twelve months_:--gelatine, arrow-root, and water, as\r
#Text=above; 1¼ pints of milk; 1½ or 2 gills of cream.
#Text=If the child is feeble, use in each case one quart of water.
#Text=Put the gelatine into 1¼ pints of hot water, and when it boils add\r
#Text=the arrow-root dissolved in a gill of cold water.
#Text=When this has boiled\r
#Text=five minutes, add the milk, and when it boils again pour in the cream.
#Text=Take it from the fire, and sweeten with loaf sugar until it is slightly\r
#Text=sweeter than cow's milk.
#Text=Strain if necessary, through fine muslin,\r
#Text=and stir occasionally while cooling.
#Text=If the child is constipated, use\r
#Text=a little more cream, or sweeten with brown sugar.
#Text=In the opposite\r
#Text=case, use a little less cream.
#Text=This food should be prepared once in\r
#Text=twenty-four hours; in warm weather, twice, unless kept in a very cool\r
#Text=place.
#Text=MISCELLANEOUS RECEIPTS AND DIRECTIONS.
#Text==English Ginger Beer.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Pour four quarts of boiling water, upon an ounce and a half of ginger,\r
#Text=an ounce of cream of tartar, a pound of clean brown sugar, and two\r
#Text=fresh lemons, sliced thin.
#Text=It should be wrought twenty-four hours,\r
#Text=with two gills of good yeast, and then bottled.
#Text=It improves by keeping\r
#Text=several weeks, unless the weather is hot, and it is an excellent\r
#Text=beverage.
#Text=If made with loaf instead of brown sugar, the appearance and\r
#Text=flavor are still finer.
#Text==Maple Beer.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To four gallons of boiling water, add one quart of maple syrup and a\r
#Text=small table-spoonful of essence of spruce.
#Text=When it is about milk warm,\r
#Text=add a pint of yeast; and when fermented bottle it.
#Text=In three days it is\r
#Text=fit for use.
#Text==Spring Beer.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a handful of checkerberry (wintergreen), a few sassafras roots\r
#Text=cut up, a half a handful of pine-buds, while they are small and gummy,\r
#Text=and a small handful of hops.[18] Put all these into a pail of water\r
#Text=over night, and in the morning boil them two or three hours; fill up\r
#Text=the kettle when it boils away.
#Text=Strain it into a jar or firkin that\r
#Text=will hold a half a pailful more of water.
#Text=Stir in a pint and a half\r
#Text=of molasses, then add the half pailful of water, and taste it.
#Text=If not\r
#Text=sweet enough add more molasses.
#Text=It loses the sweetness a little in the\r
#Text=process of fermentation, and should therefore be made rather too sweet\r
#Text=at first.
#Text=Add two or three gills of good yeast, set it in a warm place,\r
#Text=and let it remain undisturbed till it is fermented.
#Text=When the top is\r
#Text=covered with a thick dark foam, take it off; have ready clean bottles\r
#Text=and good corks; pour off the beer into another vessel, so gently as not\r
#Text=to disturb the sediment; then bottle it, and set it in a cool place.
#Text=It\r
#Text=will be ready for use in two days.
#Text=The sediment should be put into a\r
#Text=bottle by itself, loosely corked, and kept to ferment the next brewing
#Text=.
#Text=[18] If dried in the ordinary way.
#Text=But a small pinch of the hops put up\r
#Text= in pound packages by the Shakers is enough.
#Text==Spruce and Boneset Beer.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil a small handful each of hops and boneset for an hour or two, in a\r
#Text=pailful of water; strain it, and dilute it with cold water till it is\r
#Text=of the right strength.
#Text=Add a small table-spoonful of essence of spruce,\r
#Text=sweeten, ferment and bottle it, like the spring beer.
#Text=The essences of hops, checkerberry, ginger, and spruce, put into warm\r
#Text=water in suitable proportions, then sweetened, fermented, and bottled,\r
#Text=make good beer.
#Text==Rennet Wine.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash a third, or half of a salted rennet; wipe it dry and put it into a\r
#Text=bottle of wine.
#Text=The wine will be fit to use for custard the next day.
#Text=To keep the remainder of the rennet till more is needed, put it into a\r
#Text=strong brine and cover it close.
#Text==To Boil Cider.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take cider which has been made but a day or two, and boil it nearly\r
#Text=half away.
#Text=Skim it often.
#Text=It will keep good a long time, and is useful\r
#Text=in making mince pies, and to flavor pudding sauce.
#Text=Bottle it and cork\r
#Text=it well.
#Text=A mould will form over the top, but will not injure the cider.
#Text==Cologne Water.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To one gallon of alcohol, put twelve drachms each, of oil of lavender,\r
#Text=oil of bergamot, and essence of lemon; four drachms of oil of rosemary,\r
#Text=and twelve drops of oil of cinnamon.
#Text==Indelible Ink.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To make the ink, put into the small bottle six cents worth of lunar\r
#Text=caustic, and fill it with rain water.
#Text=To make the wash, nearly fill the largest bottle with soft water, and\r
#Text=add gum arabic enough to make a thin solution--about a teaspoonful of\r
#Text=the lumps.
#Text=Then put in a drachm of salt of tartar.
#Text=If the ink spreads,\r
#Text=add more gum-arabic to the wash.
#Text==To prevent Books, Ink, Paste, &c., from moulding.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=A drop or two of oil of lavender on a book, and a single one in a pint\r
#Text=bottle of ink, will prevent mould.
#Text==Tooth Powder.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Two ounces of Peruvian bark, two of myrrh, one of chalk, one of\r
#Text=Armenian bole, and one of orris root.
#Text==Rose Butter= (a good substitute for rose water).
#Text=Gather every morning the leaves of the roses that blossomed the day\r
#Text=before, and put them in a stone jar in alternate layers with fine salt.
#Text=After all the leaves are gathered, put a saucer or small plate into the\r
#Text=jar, and lay in a pound of butter, for cake or pudding sauce.
#Text=It is a\r
#Text=very good way of obtaining the flavor of roses, without expense.
#Text==To keep Parsley.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Gather fresh sprigs, and after washing them, chop them fine, and work\r
#Text=them into as much butter as will be needed for boiled poultry, lamb,\r
#Text=and fish, before the next summer.
#Text=Put the butter into a stone jar, and\r
#Text=cover it with a brine made with nice salt.
#Text==To keep Suet.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Pull off the skin or membrane from fresh suet, sprinkle salt upon it,\r
#Text=tie it up in a cloth or bag, and hang it in a cool, dry place.
#Text=It will\r
#Text=keep sweet the year round.
#Text==To keep Eggs.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=To four quarts of air-slacked lime, put two ounces of cream of tartar\r
#Text=(that is, two table-spoonfuls), two of salt, and four quarts of cold\r
#Text=water.
#Text=Put fresh eggs into a stone jar, and pour the mixture over them.
#Text=This will keep nine dozen, provided they are all good when laid down;\r
#Text=and after many months, the yolks will be still whole, and the whites\r
#Text=stiff and clear as at first.
#Text=The water may settle away so as to leave\r
#Text=the upper layer uncovered.
#Text=If so, add more.
#Text=Cover them closely and keep\r
#Text=them in a cool place.
#Text=Eggs should be laid down when they are at the lowest market price.
#Text==To cleanse a Calf's Head and Feet.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take them as soon as the animal is killed, wash them clean, and in\r
#Text=order to remove the hair, sprinkle pulverized rosin over them and dip\r
#Text=them for an instant in scalding water.
#Text=The rosin will dry immediately,\r
#Text=and they can be easily scraped clean.
#Text=Soak them from one to three days\r
#Text=in cold water, changing it repeatedly.
#Text==To kill Cockroaches and Beetles.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Strew the roots of black hellebore, at night, in the places infested\r
#Text=by these vermin, and they will be found in the morning dead, or dying.
#Text=Black hellebore grows in marshy grounds, and may be had at the herb\r
#Text=shops.
#Text==To drive away Ants.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The little red ants will leave closets where sea-sand is sprinkled, or\r
#Text=where oyster shells are laid.
#Text=Scatter sprigs of wormwood in places infested with black ants.
#Text==To secure Woollens, Furs, Furniture, etc., from Moths.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Carefully shake and brush woollens early in the spring, so as to be\r
#Text=certain that no eggs are in them; then sew them up in cotton or linen\r
#Text=wrappers, putting a piece of camphor gum, tied up in a bit of muslin,\r
#Text=into each bundle, or into the chests and closets where the articles are\r
#Text=to lie.
#Text=When the gum is evaporated it must be renewed.
#Text=A lady put up her blankets and carpets in this way before going to\r
#Text=Europe, and on her return, three or four years after, found every\r
#Text=article safe from moths.
#Text=Furs should not be hung out in the sun in the spring before being put\r
#Text=away for the season.
#Text=The moth miller will be likely to visit them when\r
#Text=thus exposed.
#Text=They should be put into a close box with a piece of\r
#Text=camphor, and the box tied up in a pillow case or bag.
#Text=Blankets that are in use only occasionally during the summer, should be\r
#Text=laid when not wanted, under a mattress in constant use, or in a trunk\r
#Text=where there are pieces of camphor gum, or cedar chips.
#Text=It would be a\r
#Text=most convenient arrangement for housekeepers to have a closet with\r
#Text=shelves and draws made of cedar boards.
#Text=It is more difficult than it used to be, to preserve woollens, furs,\r
#Text=carpets, and furniture from being injured by moths.
#Text=Thirty years since\r
#Text=it was regarded as an indication of very negligent housekeeping to have\r
#Text=a moth-eaten carpet.
#Text=Now, the utmost care will not always preserve\r
#Text=carpets from being injured in this way.
#Text=Perhaps the reason may be, that\r
#Text=in general, warehouses and dwellings are warmed throughout, during the\r
#Text=winter, by furnaces.
#Text=New stuffed and cushioned furniture is sometimes\r
#Text=found to contain moths.
#Text=To destroy them, pour burning fluid plentifully\r
#Text=upon the cushions, sofas, &c.
#Text=If it is fresh, it will leave no stain,\r
#Text=and the disagreeable odor will soon pass away.
#Text=To preserve a carpet\r
#Text=that cannot be often shaken, draw out the tacks twice a year, turn back\r
#Text=the edges a quarter of a yard all around, brush out the dust, and then\r
#Text=with a painter's brush put new spirits of turpentine upon the boards\r
#Text=as far as the carpet is turned back; then return it immediately to its\r
#Text=place, and put in the tacks.
#Text=The floors of some houses have moths in the cracks.
#Text=In this case,\r
#Text=cedar saw-dust sprinkled over the floor before laying down the carpet,\r
#Text=will protect it from these diligent mischief-workers.
#Text=If this cannot be\r
#Text=had use tar-paper.
#Text==To kill Moths.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take furs or pillows infested with moths, and put them into a brick\r
#Text=oven which has just been used for baking.
#Text=Let them remain over night,\r
#Text=and the next day beat them well in the open air.
#Text==To remove the Bad Odor from New Feathers.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Make a cover for the bed of some coarse material, or a couple of old\r
#Text=sheets; get a baker to put it into his oven one or two nights.
#Text=A better\r
#Text=way, when it can be done, is to send the feathers in bags to a baker's\r
#Text=oven, before they are put into the tick.
#Text==To purify a Sink or Drain.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Dissolve half a pound of copperas in two gallons of water, and pour in\r
#Text=half one day, and the remainder the next.
#Text==To take out Mildew.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=(This and the next receipt were furnished by a chemist.)
#Text=Obtain the dryest chloride of lime that can be bought, and for strong\r
#Text=fabrics dissolve four table-spoonfuls in a half a pint of water.
#Text=Let\r
#Text=the mildewed article lie fifteen minutes in this solution.
#Text=Then take\r
#Text=it out, wring it gently, and put it immediately into weak muriatic\r
#Text=acid--one part of the acid and four parts soft water.
#Text=For delicate fabrics, laces, muslins, &c., the solution of lime should\r
#Text=be diluted by the addition of three or four times the measure of water.
#Text=Let the article lie in it five minutes; then put it into the muriatic\r
#Text=acid.
#Text==To take out Iron Mould.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Dissolve a teaspoonful of salts of tin in two table-spoonfuls of\r
#Text=water.
#Text=Dip the iron-mould into the solution, and let it remain five\r
#Text=minutes.
#Text=Then dip it into a mixture of equal parts of muriatic acid and\r
#Text=water.
#Text=Dip the mould spots alternately into these mixtures, or make the\r
#Text=first one stronger with the salts of tin, and apply it with a soft rag\r
#Text=on the end of a stick.
#Text=Last of all, rinse the articles very thoroughly\r
#Text=in cold water.
#Text=A simpler method of removing iron-mould succeeds well, provided it is\r
#Text=recent, and not very dark.
#Text=Tie up a teaspoonful of cream of tartar in\r
#Text=the moulded place, and put it into cold water without soap, and boil it\r
#Text=half an hour.
#Text==To take out Ink.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Turn boiling water upon it immediately, in this way: spread the cloth\r
#Text=over a pitcher or basin, with the ink-spots in the centre, and while\r
#Text=you hold it in its place, let another person turn the boiling water on\r
#Text=the spots.
#Text=This is better than to put the article into boiling water,\r
#Text=as the whole will then be tinged with the ink.
#Text=If the spots are still\r
#Text=visible, tie up a teaspoonful of cream of tartar in the places where\r
#Text=they are--more for a large stain, less for a very small one--then put\r
#Text=the cloth into cold water without soap, and boil it half an hour.
#Text=If it\r
#Text=is not convenient to put boiling water at once on the stains, put them\r
#Text=in cold water; do not let them become dry.
#Text=Articles that have been stained with ink or fruit, should not be put\r
#Text=into soap suds until the stains are removed.
#Text=Soap will tend to make\r
#Text=them permanent.
#Text==To take out Fruit Stains.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Tie up cream of tartar in the spots, and put the cloth in cold water,\r
#Text=to boil; or if the stains are much spread, stir the cream of tartar\r
#Text=into the water.
#Text=If they are still visible, boil the cloth in a mixture\r
#Text=of subcarbonate of soda a small table-spoonful to a pail of water.
#Text==To take out Grease or Fresh Paint.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Rub grease spots with chloric ether.
#Text=To remove paint, the ether\r
#Text=should be applied on the other side.
#Text=The ether carries off the oil by\r
#Text=evaporation, and leaves the lead of which the paint was composed, dry.
#Text=New turpentine will remove fresh paint.
#Text==To remove rust from Iron Ware and Stoves.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=New stove or range furniture is sometimes so much rusted as to make the\r
#Text=use of it very inconvenient.
#Text=Put into a rusty kettle as much hay as it\r
#Text=will hold, fill it with water and boil it many hours.
#Text=At night set it\r
#Text=aside, and the next day boil it again.
#Text=If it is not entirely fit for\r
#Text=use, repeat the process.
#Text=It will certainly be effectual.
#Text=Rub the rusty spots on a stove with sand-paper, and then with sweet oil.
#Text==To take off starch or rust from Flat-irons.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Tie up a piece of yellow beeswax in a rag, and when the iron is almost,\r
#Text=but not quite hot enough to use, rub it quickly with the wax, and then\r
#Text=with a coarse cloth.
#Text==To prevent Glass, Earthen, Potter's and Iron Ware from being easily\r
#Text=broken.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put dishes, tumblers, and other glass articles into a kettle; cover\r
#Text=them entirely with cold water, and put the kettle where it will soon\r
#Text=boil.
#Text=When it has boiled a few minutes, set it aside, covered close.
#Text=When the water is cold, take out the glass.
#Text=Treat new earthen ware in the same way.
#Text=When potter's ware is boiled, a\r
#Text=handful or two of bran should be thrown into the water, and the glazing\r
#Text=will never be injured by acids or salt.
#Text=Cast-iron stoves, and iron ware should be heated gradually the first\r
#Text=time they are used.
#Text==A permanent Cement for Glass, China, and Wood.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Steep Russian Isinglass twenty-four hours in white brandy, gently boil\r
#Text=and stir the mixture until it is well compounded, and a drop of it,\r
#Text=cooled, will become a very thick jelly; then strain it through a linen\r
#Text=cloth, and cork it up closely.
#Text=A gentle heat will dissolve it into a\r
#Text=colorless fluid.
#Text=Broken dishes united with it, will break elsewhere,\r
#Text=rather than separate in the old fracture.
#Text=To apply it, rub the edges,\r
#Text=place them together, and hold them two or three minutes.
#Text==To preserve steel Knives from Rust.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Never wrap them in woollen cloths.
#Text=When they are not to be used for\r
#Text=some time, have them made bright, and perfectly dry; then take a soft\r
#Text=rag, and rub each blade with dry wood-ashes.
#Text=Wrap them closely in thick\r
#Text=brown paper and lay them in a drawer or dry closet.
#Text=A set of elegant\r
#Text=knives, used only on great occasions, were kept in this way more than\r
#Text=an hundred years without a spot of rust.
#Text==To prevent Ivory Knife Handles from being cracked.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Never let knife blades stand in hot water as is sometimes done to make\r
#Text=them wash easily.
#Text=The heat expands the steel which runs up into the\r
#Text=handle a very little, and this cracks the ivory.
#Text=Knife handles should\r
#Text=never lie in water.
#Text=A handsome knife, or one used for cooking is soon\r
#Text=spoiled in this way.
#Text==To remove spots from Furniture.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Paint or white spots occasioned by spilling medicine or setting\r
#Text=something hot upon furniture, can be removed by rubbing them with\r
#Text=camphene.
#Text==To remove Mortar or Paint from Windows.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Rub the spots of mortar with a stiff brush dipped in sharp, hot\r
#Text=vinegar, and paint spots with burning fluid or camphene and sand.
#Text==To clean Paint with Pumice-stone.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Use powdered pumice-stone instead of whiting or sand.
#Text=It cleans paint\r
#Text=very quickly, and without injuring it.
#Text=But very little should be put\r
#Text=on the cloth at once.
#Text=A pint of it is enough to clean the paint of a\r
#Text=large house.
#Text=It is well to keep it on hand, as it is often needed for\r
#Text=removing spots from paint, and for cleaning closet shelves.
#Text==To polish unvarnished Mahogany Furniture.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=First take out ink stains, if there are any, by touching them with\r
#Text=spirits of salt.
#Text=Do it with a sponge tied upon the end of a stick; then\r
#Text=wash the spots instantly with vinegar, and make the whole surface to be\r
#Text=polished, clean with it.
#Text=Then rub on the following preparation with a\r
#Text=woollen cloth:--\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Melt together in an earthen pot two ounces of beeswax, and half an\r
#Text=ounce of alconet root; then take it from the fire and add two ounces of\r
#Text=spirits of wine, and half a pint of spirits of turpentine.
#Text=Polish with\r
#Text=a soft silk cloth.
#Text==To clean Silver and Plated Ware.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Use fine whiting, and wet it with hartshorn instead of water.
#Text=The spots\r
#Text=that make their appearance upon silver or plated-ware that is not in\r
#Text=constant use, are quickly removed by this mixture.
#Text=Silver is injured\r
#Text=by coarse whiting; therefore it is well to sift it through a piece of\r
#Text=muslin.
#Text=It is a good way to boil half an ounce of hartshorn powder in a pint of\r
#Text=water, and put into it clean linen or cotton rags enough to absorb the\r
#Text=whole of the mixture; then dry them, and keep them to clean silver and\r
#Text=plate.
#Text=Wash leather should be used afterwards.
#Text==To clean Paper Hangings.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put a clean soft bag, or an old pillow-case over a new broom, and\r
#Text=gently brush the dust from the paper; then take crusts of stale bakers'\r
#Text=bread, and wipe it down lightly, beginning at the top.
#Text=If you rub it,\r
#Text=the dirt will adhere to the paper.
#Text=After thus brushing all around the\r
#Text=upper part of the walls with the bread, begin just above where you left\r
#Text=off, and go round again.
#Text=Do thus until you have finished the paper.
#Text=The\r
#Text=dust and crumbs will fall together.
#Text=Whenever a room is cleaned it is a\r
#Text=good way, before the paint and windows are washed, to wipe the paper\r
#Text=with a covered broom as above directed.
#Text==To prepare earth for House Plants.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put together equal parts of the three following things--soil from\r
#Text=the sides of a barn-yard, well-rotted manure, and leaf mould from the\r
#Text=woods, or earth from the inside of an old tree or stump.
#Text=Add a small\r
#Text=quantity of sand.
#Text=For Cactuses, put as much sand as of the other\r
#Text=materials and a little fine charcoal.
#Text==To raise Hyacinths in Winter.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=When they are put into the glasses or earth, set them into a dark\r
#Text=closet until they sprout.
#Text=If they are in glasses, do not let the water\r
#Text=touch the bulb, by an inch.
#Text=When the roots have shot down to the water,\r
#Text=fill the glass, put in a piece of charcoal, and set them in the sun.
#Text==Soot Tea for Roses.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Get soot from a stove or chimney where wood is used for fuel, put it\r
#Text=into an old pitcher, and pour hot water upon it.
#Text=When cool, use it\r
#Text=to water your plants every few days.
#Text=When it is all used, fill up\r
#Text=the pitcher again with hot water.
#Text=The effect upon plants, especially\r
#Text=upon roses that have almost hopelessly deteriorated is wonderful in\r
#Text=producing a rapid growth of thrifty shoots, with large thick leaves,\r
#Text=and a great number of richly-tinted roses.
#Text=Never despair of a decayed\r
#Text=rose till this has been tried.
#Text==To destroy Grass in Gravel Walks.=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Scatter the cheapest coarse salt along the edges, and where-ever the\r
#Text=grass is springing.
#Text=Even the Canada thistle can be rooted out by cutting off the stalks\r
#Text=very near, but not below the surface of the ground, and putting salt on\r
#Text=them.
#Text=Old brine, not fit for any other purpose, is good for this.
